Collection › Song Dynasty › ro-song-014 — Pair of Jizhou Vases with Leaf Impressions

Reference Object ro-song-014 — Pair (A & B)

Pair of Black-Glazed Vases with Naturalistic Leaf Impressions

吉州窑 葉紋黑釉瓶 — Southern Song Dynasty, 1127–1279 CE — Jizhou Kilns, Jiangxi Province

Southern Song Jizhou Ware Leaf Impression Botanical Resist Black Iron Glaze Chan Aesthetic Monumental Form Rare Type GOM Examined
Exceptional rarity: While Jizhou kilns are internationally celebrated for tea bowls with leaf impressions, large ceremonial vases employing the same botanical resist technique are extremely uncommon. This pair represents an important typological extension of the Jizhou leaf-impression tradition beyond tea ceramics to sculptural vessel forms intended for elite scholarly or ritual settings.

Object Description

An exceptional pair of monumental black-glazed stoneware vases from the Jizhou kilns in Jiangxi Province, each decorated with a single naturalistic botanical leaf impression produced by the authentic one-fire resist technique. Both vessels are covered with a thick iron-rich black glaze fired in a reducing atmosphere above approximately 1260°C, producing the characteristic deep black vitreous matrix. Each leaf impression appears as an amber-golden mineral positive on the black glaze ground, fully encapsulated beneath the transparent upper glaze layer.

The two vases present distinct forms: Vase A is a high-shouldered baluster with paired strap handles evoking archaic bronze hu vessels; Vase B is an elegant slender bottle in the yuhuchun tradition expressing restrained Song literati aesthetics. Both share the same glaze technology, leaf-impression technique, and naturally developed crackle network.

The deliberate asymmetric placement of the leaf on each vessel — a single element on an otherwise undecorated surface — embodies core Chan philosophical principles: balance through controlled irregularity, the power of negative space, and harmony between natural form and human craft. No two authentic leaf impressions are ever identical, as each uses a unique botanical specimen that becomes permanently part of the manufacturing process.

Jizhou Leaf-Impression Technique

The leaf-impression decoration is produced through a sophisticated one-fire botanical resist process that cannot be replicated by surface painting or transfer printing. GOM examination confirms that the leaf participated directly in the original firing rather than being added after manufacture.

One-Fire Botanical Resist — Manufacturing Sequence

1

Leaf Application

A fresh natural botanical leaf is applied directly to the leather-hard clay body before glazing.

2

Glazing

The iron-rich black glaze is applied over the entire surface including the leaf, which acts as a resist masking the body beneath.

3

Firing

At approximately 1260°C in a reducing atmosphere, the organic leaf material combusts completely.

4

Mineralisation

The combustion products modify the local glaze chemistry, leaving a permanent amber-gold mineral trace encapsulated beneath the glaze.

5

Result

The botanical venation is permanently preserved as a sub-glaze mineral image. Each vessel is literally unique.

Microscopic Diagnostic Analysis

Both vases were examined using Gemmological Optical Microscopy (GOM) at magnifications from 20× to 100×. The examination focused on leaf venation morphology, glaze–leaf interface, combustion boundary characteristics, crackle network, and glaze microstructure. Observations were compared with published analytical studies of authenticated Southern Song Jizhou wares.

GOM Documentation — Leaf Impression Microstructure

~30×

Fig. 3. Vase A — Leaf impression at moderate magnification. Complete primary and secondary venation hierarchy preserved. The dark combustion boundary separates the amber mineral leaf image from the iron-black glaze matrix. The smooth transparent amber glass envelope encapsulating the impression is clearly visible at the margins.

~25×

Fig. 4. Vase B — Leaf impression detail. Botanical venation with parallel secondary vein structure. The mineral replacement texture within the leaf field and the continuity of the fine crackle network across both the leaf zone and surrounding black glaze confirm simultaneous formation during the original firing.

GOM Documentation — Glaze and Crackle Morphology

~40×

Fig. 5. Neck crackle network. The fine naturally developed crackle propagates continuously across the neck surface. A reddish iron-rich band is visible at the glaze–body junction, consistent with iron migration during high-temperature reduction firing. Age-consistent mineral infill in crack channels.

1. Leaf Venation and Combustion Boundary

GOM examination of both vases documents the complete venation hierarchy of the botanical leaves: primary midrib, secondary lateral veins, and tertiary venation are preserved in the amber mineral trace. The dark combustion boundary separating the amber leaf image from the surrounding iron-black glaze records the precise outline of the original botanical specimen. This boundary shows a gradual mineral transition rather than a sharp painted edge, confirming authentic in-firing formation rather than post-firing surface application or transfer printing.

2. Sub-Glaze Encapsulation

The amber leaf images are fully encapsulated beneath a transparent glaze layer rather than sitting on the glaze surface. This internal encapsulation is the primary criterion distinguishing authentic one-fire botanical resist decoration from post-firing painted or transfer-printed imitations. The smooth amber glass envelope surrounding the leaf impression demonstrates that the modified glaze chemistry at the leaf contact zone vitrified normally during firing, sealing the botanical mineral trace beneath the upper glaze.

3. Mineral Replacement Texture

Within the amber leaf fields, microscopy reveals the mineral replacement texture produced when the organic leaf material combusted and its mineral content modified the local glaze chemistry. This texture has an irregular, naturalistic character without mechanical regularity — consistent with the variable mineral content of a real botanical specimen — and cannot be reproduced by artificial means. The colour gradation within the leaf field (deeper amber at the veins, lighter golden at the interveinal areas) reflects the differential mineral density of leaf tissue.

4. Crackle Network Continuity

The fine natural crackle network propagates continuously across both the leaf impression zones and the surrounding iron-black glaze, confirming that both the leaf image and the crackle formed during the same single firing event. A reddish iron-rich band at the glaze–body junction on the neck records iron migration during high-temperature reduction firing, consistent with authentic Jizhou kiln technology.

5. Glaze Composition (Comparative)

The observed glaze optical properties are consistent with published analytical data for authenticated Southern Song Jizhou wares showing compositions of SiO₂ 60–66 wt.%, Fe₂O₃ 5–6 wt.%, Al₂O₃ 16–20 wt.%, CaO 4–8 wt.%, and MnO approximately 1 wt.%, fired in a strongly reducing atmosphere above 1260°C.

ComponentPublished Range (Jizhou)
SiO₂60–66 wt.%
Fe₂O₃5–6 wt.%
Al₂O₃16–20 wt.%
CaO4–8 wt.%
MnO~1 wt.%

Diagnostic Interpretation

Based on macroscopic examination, GOM, stylistic comparison, glaze morphology, crackle development, and published archaeometric data, both vases present a coherent diagnostic profile consistent with original production at the Jizhou kilns during the 12th–13th centuries. The coexistence of authentic botanical venation preservation, sub-glaze encapsulation, genuine combustion boundary morphology, mineral replacement texture, and age-consistent crackle with mineral infill provides strong visual and technological authentication evidence.

Key Diagnostic Markers (Both Vases)

Sub-glaze encapsulation of leaf impression (not surface-applied)
Complete botanical venation hierarchy preserved (primary, secondary, tertiary)
Gradual combustion boundary — mineral transition, not painted edge
Mineral replacement texture within leaf field (variable, naturalistic)
Amber glass envelope encapsulating leaf margins
Crackle network continuous across leaf zone and surrounding glaze
Iron-rich reddish band at glaze–body junction (reduction firing)
Dense high-fired stoneware body
Natural glaze flow and pooling around vessel forms
No evidence of transfer decoration, painted outlines, or post-firing modification
High internal diagnostic consistency — both vases

Diagnostic Confidence: High — Catalogue Nos. SS-JZ-LF-VS-01A–B
